meta.title: Mapping the Real World With Solana

transcript:
Danny Nelson: Welcome to the stage. So I want to get things off right away with an interesting comparison. So I was just with my cousin, he is a community manager for Waze. What he does is he brings people together in different parts of the world, uh, and encourages them through like little gifts and little trips, hotel stays, to help Waze map the world. See where stoplights are, see all this. And Waze is one of the biggest mapping companies in the world. They have a community, they're bootstrapping a community to get this information. Now, what my question for you to start off, how does a token make this better?

Ariel Seidman: Yeah. Um, well token works in terms of incentivizing people to buy, uh, our camera. And so, just really quickly, well, thank you for having me, appreciate it. Um, what, you buy this camera from us and it looks like, looks and smells like a dashcam, right? So like any dashcam you get in an Uber, Lyft car, I'm sure you guys have all seen them. And, but what that dashcam is doing in addition to providing like safety and all that type of stuff, it's actually mapping the world. So it has eyes, in other words the cameras, and it has AI intelligence that is doing many of the similar things that people in Waze do behind a computer, right? So, okay, like, how many lanes are on this road? Um, that speed limit sign over there, that's 25 miles an hour. There's a no turn restriction over here, you're not allowed to make a left between 4:00 and 6:00 PM. Two of the three lanes are actually have road construction right now. All that type of stuff is actually being mapped and, uh, on the device level and then we're just uploading the bits and parts. The big problem with Waze, I love Waze, I think they, when I was at Yahoo Maps, uh, I was running it for a while, it was like, look, like I could compare myself to Google Maps, but fundamentally like both of those products got people from point A to point B, right? And so people would be like, how are you better than Google Maps? Whatever, I'd make up a bunch of crap. In retrospect, like they were fundamentally the same product, right? Then Waze came around, I had already left at that point, like 2010, 2012, and all of a sudden Google Maps and Yahoo for that matter, like felt incredibly stale, right? Like that was like a wow moment for me, right? It was like, okay, you, you just, I mean you guys all use Waze now, but like at the time, like it just felt like that product was so much more interesting, so much fresher, so much more useful. Our belief is that with vision, in other words cameras at the edge, uh, combined with AI obviously, that that's a kind of another leapfrog, right? Because fundamentally what's happening with Waze? So Waze is basically asking a human to interpret what they see and the number, and then you punch it in while you're driving 65 miles an hour down the road, you're like, okay, there's a cop here, you know. Um, and, and so, but the other thing aspect of it is, in addition to being unsafe, is that it's only asking you for a very small sliver of things, right? But with cameras and AI, like you're interpreting and understanding all those things, right? And so, kind of a longer answer to your question, but I appreciate that your cousin is still doing that. I didn't realize that people are still doing that. Waze got bought by Google for those of you guys don't know.

Danny Nelson: Yeah. So another question on this topic. Uh, DePIN, Decentralized Physical Infrastructure. This idea that you can create like actual, uh, you could put hardware out into the world, uh, and have network effects and do amazing things with crypto incentives. Is it living up to the hype?

Ariel Seidman: Um, okay, there's two parts of that answer. One is in terms of markets, probably not, right? Let's be really honest. Uh, in terms of like, in terms of crypto, let me, let me sharpen that actually. In term, if you go to like whatever CoinMarketCap and you look up like our token or like, you know, all the other majors, right, in the DePIN category, Helium, GEODNET, I think the answer to that is probably no, right? But in terms of actually delivering value like to customers, like yes, 100%, right? So Lyft is our customer, Volkswagen is our customer, HERE Technologies which is owned by BMW, Audi, etc. is our customer. Uh, NBC is our customer, Ricoh is our customer. I mean the list goes on, right? And so they're all delivering tremendous value to their products and services through our data. Um, Helium, which I know those guys very well, I think they have like 2 million people using their network in some form or fashion, right? Hundreds of thousands of mobile subscribers. So like for a startup that's incredible, right? But in terms of the crypto markets, like they're like golf clap is basically what you get, right? And so I do think that's a fundamental issue right now that we're facing in the DePIN category. It's a real problem.

Danny Nelson: Help us understand what is the role that the, the blockchain actually plays in delivering this? Like why, not just why Solana, like what are the attributes of Solana, but why has, why, why is it necessary to have this sort of really valuable infrastructure? Like it makes sense to have people go out to the world and collect this data. It's useful, it, uh, it just works. But where do blockchains come into play and why are they necessary?

Ariel Seidman: Well, there's, there's a couple. One is the incentive mechanism, which I think is important, right? We talked about that a little bit. Um, the other one is coordination, right? So like we need to be able to coordinate a lot of people in a lot of different parts of the world and it needs to be trustworthy. So we actually started off not paying people with our crypto token. We just started paying people cash, okay? So this is like 2019 timeframe. And it was working, okay, to be clear. Um, but we said a couple of things. One is like people start to like point their camera up at the sky and then we wouldn't pay them, right? And we'd be like, we're not mapping the sky, we're mapping the road, you know? And they'd be like, I'm going to report you to the Better Business Bureau, you guys suck, whatever, all this type of crap, right? And then the other thing is we wanted to basically pay people very quickly, right? So these people in the Philippines would be like, can you just pay us in crypto, right? So that was another like kind of interesting. Anyways, long story short is like there's the crypto incentive which we think is pretty strong and it can be really important. Um, the other part is trust, right? Like by putting all of the rewards and the consumption on the blockchain, we were, we no longer became the bad guy, right? That was like not a totally, you know, I kind of understood that at a superficial level, but I, there's, there's a lot of depth there that I think is important. And so, you know, now people look at it, be like, okay, if I'm driving in this region, how much could I make? Okay, boom, boom, boom. It's all out there. It's all out there, right? Like how much consumption is coming in. So really the people who are driving are our salespeople and there's a lot more trust in the system because people don't point the finger. I mean people still yell at me for a lot of stuff if you go on, you know, Twitter, like that's always fun, but like that's fine, that's part of the job.

Danny Nelson: Do they ever make a good point?

Ariel Seidman: Yeah, for sure. Yeah. I mean look, like I'm probably more frustrated about what they're frustrated about, right? Is the reality, right? So like if there's a bug in the product, like that pains me more than them. Like I hate that crap. And so, but yeah, there's some that is obviously like more personal in nature, but that's just the internet in general. So it's like if you don't want to do that, don't go on the internet, don't go on a Twitter account, you know?

Danny Nelson: To jump back for a second, you mentioned something really interesting, which is that it sounds like you almost backed into crypto. You were building a solution and then realized that the thing you were building would just work better on crypto rails. So dig a little deeper on that.

Ariel Seidman: Yeah, I mean, and a thousand, I mean look, when we were doing it by cash, like we maybe had like 1% of the global road network mapped. Now we've mapped 36%. So it has like definitively worked, you know, like there's a thousand percent. Now I will say the following though, which is, um, so we've mapped 36% of the global road network, which is great, amazing. Uh, a lot of our customers are like, we need more coverage. Like a lot more coverage, right? And we need, you know, coverage in Brazil. We don't have a lot of coverage in Brazil, right? We want coverage in Vietnam or, you know, whatever, all these different countries. Uh, and so the thing that I think is important now is, look, you'll always have like the crypto incentivized folks, right? These guys, another part of your question I think. You always have the crypto incentivized folks. But the reality is, is like even if our token price was like a dollar right now, which is not, um, like there is not enough crypto, the, the population or the audience that we would be talking to around the crypto incentives is not big enough to satisfy the kind of coverage that we need, right? Like if, if the crypto industry was like 10x, then I think yes, we could, and our crypto, and our token was a dollar, then there's enough of an audience that we can talk to and basically be like, here's a camera, you install it, you earn these tokens, blah blah, right? Like it's all very straightforward. But the reality is, is that the crypto industry is not that big yet. And so what we're now thinking about is, okay, what are the, what are the products and the technologies which will take this camera and get into a lot more devices regardless of whether or not there's a crypto token attached to it, right? And so like commercial fleets is very interesting to us, right? Commercial fleets, if you're a trucking company, your delivery company, whatever it is, you may have noticed there's like a little dashcam in there. What are they doing? They're monitoring that driver to say like, okay, which of those drivers are driving like an idiot, right? Because that's a big liability for me, right? Like insurance wise, you know, God forbid they kill somebody driving like an idiot. Um, and they just want to know where their drivers are. Like, hey, did you hit that route at that customer at that right time, all that type of stuff, right? That's very valuable information for them. And so we have customers that are basically pay us to be, you know, we provide them a service, right? All that type of stuff that I just mentioned. But in, but in addition to that, they're also mapping. But they don't know about the crypto token. Like they don't care about it. I mean some of them do, but like the vast majority, you say crypto token, they're like, ah, what's up? You know, like, I don't care, you take it. Um, and so, and there's some other stuff that we'll probably announce in 2026 along the same lines of getting more coverage in a, in a, in a method that doesn't rely only on the crypto token.

Danny Nelson: So that, that's really interesting. I haven't actually thought about that before. This fact that the crypto tokens aren't, and incentive programs aren't going far enough. Like it, it works for some areas, it works for what is it, 36% you said?

Ariel Seidman: 36%, yeah, 36% of the global road network.

Danny Nelson: 36% of the global road network, but I don't know, there's some backwater road, some like in DePIN, uh, Patagonia that just isn't getting mapped because no one who's in on crypto lives anywhere close to there.

Ariel Seidman: Exactly, exactly. Yeah. So, so if the entire, kind of going back to that point of like, if the entire industry was 10x, you know, bigger, then yeah, there's some little small town in France or Brazil where we don't have coverage yet that somebody heard about us through this conference or some, some, you know, another kind of crypto audience, but that person doesn't exist, right? In some of these small towns. So my whole big thing is like, you know, the crypto industry needs to get bigger, a lot bigger. Um, and my view is, and this may be a little bit controversial sitting inside a Solana conference, is like, I don't think the way we're going to get there is with more casinos, right? Like I think we need more products that are going to touch more users in the, in the real world. I mean look, why is it that like, you know, products like ChatGPT and Claude and all, you know, Gemini, whatever it is, they're growing so quickly in the AI world is because they're fundamentally useful products, right? And so people are just using them because they're useful products. I mean you guys all use them. And so if there was just more useful things, the crypto industry would very quickly get a lot bigger.

Danny Nelson: How do we find more useful things?

Ariel Seidman: Uh, that's the magic question. I don't know. Uh, I mean I have some ideas. I think part of it is a structural problem and part of it is a cultural problem, right? There's a structural problem which is, look, if you create a casino, you can make a lot of money very quickly, right? I think we all know that. Whereas some of these other products, they take longer to, to evolve and grow. I mean SpaceX started in 2002, right? I mean it will probably go public next year, be worth whatever, 1 trillion or 1.5 trillion, massive. But that was what, 23, you know, 23 years, 24 years? Long, long time. Like crypto world, like they move like within weeks. Like, ooh, that's hot, that's hot, you know? Very, very different than like, uh, that world which is a little bit more patient with their capital, with like the idea and stuff like that. So I think that's a cultural problem. Um, well sorry, that's a structural problem. In terms of culture, look, I think you just need more people, right? Basically saying like, we want real products and services. Like right, there's a casino, like I have no issue with the casino. Sometimes I go to Vegas and I'll do blackjack or whatever it is. I got no problem with that, that's cool. But like that can't be a big chunk of like what crypto is today. And, and, you know, I would argue you have stablecoins, which are real. Like my bro, one of my brothers is very crypto skeptical, he works at Shopify and, you know, I think they'll do something around, um, hopefully that doesn't like break any like news. That would be really bad if like that's not public. But anyways.

Danny Nelson: It's all right.

Ariel Seidman: Yeah, shit. As I said it I was like, oh crap, someone's going to come after me now. Um, ouch. Uh, anyways, I hope they do something around stablecoins. Uh, so that's real, right? Like even my brother who's like a pretty big crypto skeptic is like, oh this stuff makes sense, right? Um, and then you have a bunch of stuff in DePIN, uh, and then you have exchanges, right? And then you have companies like Bitwise, stuff like that. So there, there are real products and services, but like a big, big chunk of it is, you know, some version of a casino.

Danny Nelson: So to go into where products really are useful, I think that DePIN stands out because it, it is that moment where you see something useful being created. In the case of Hivemapper, it's, uh, it's mapping software, or not, sorry, not mapping software, mapping data. In the case of, uh, Helium, it's better, better cell coverage. Um, how do you, like you look at DePIN, how does it stack up in the crypto world for you?

Ariel Seidman: I mean in terms of like enlarging the pie, which I think is the most important thing that we all have to be focused on, I think it is one, you know, other than stablecoins, the thing that is enlarging the pie, right? Because like I was saying before is like people who are coming in using Helium or people using Hivemapper data and these commercial fleets, etc., they don't, they don't care about crypto. Like they're just trying to buy a product, a better service, whatever it is. The fact that it's running on crypto rails is like besides the point, right? And so that to me is like fundamentally interesting in terms of enlarging the overall market. So, you know, this conference five years from now is, I don't know how many people are here, you know, it's 5,000, 6,000 people, that there's 50,000 people, right? And I think the way to get there is focusing, you know, all of our energies as much as possible on real products and services.

Danny Nelson: So what's next for Hivemapper?

Ariel Seidman: Um, look, I think this coverage thing is real. Like this coverage challenge that we have, I mean, you know, I think we're all very proud of the fact that we've hit 36% of the global road network. We love that. Um, and yet we need more, okay? So let me talk about this a little bit more. Uh, some of our biggest customers are saying to us like, this is great, but like you got to be like 80%, 90%, right? In terms of like coverage per se. There's another aspect of it is like freshness, right? So like let's take the metro area of Los Angeles, which is about 100,000 uh road kilometers. So it's quite large area in terms of uh road kilometers. People like to drive there. So in order for us to provide a map that is fundamentally fresher than Waze, that requires probably like daily active cameras about 5,000, right? We have a lot in LA. Um, I think it's about maybe 800 or 900 that are there, maybe a little bit more, but it's not, you know, 4 or 5,000, right? And so at 4 or 5,000 like you can provide every layer of the map, right? Like literally like an accident happens, we'll see it very quickly, right? Road construction pops up somewhere, we'll see it very quickly, right? Like all of a sudden like our vision of like a real-time map, right? Like Waze but with cameras that can see and understand everything, that starts to happen for a region like LA at like 4 or 5,000 cameras, right? So like that's the other aspect of it and so that's why we're kind of thinking in terms of like growing our coverage. Like token incentives is a, a, a critical part of it, but there has to be AND statements to that now.

Danny Nelson: And then a final question, a year from now, where do you think Hivemapper is going to be? Like number wise, is it going to be 50%? Uh, and then just in terms of the community, where is it going to be in a year?

Ariel Seidman: Um, yeah, I hope we're, I hope we're closer to 50% by the, you know, by this time next year. I would say one other thing. I think that what I think you'll start to see next year is like bigger, you know, call them Web2, Big Tech, whatever you want to, whatever, you know, name you want to put on that, I think they're going to start to like integrate into some of these, into some of these systems. Because I, I, I do think that they see the value of these decentralized networks and even for a company like Google is very expensive.

enrichment.generated_query: Ariel Seidman AI mapping dashcam Solana Breakpoint 2025 official announcement blog OR technical docs

sources.title: Introducing Contextual View in Google Maps AI Kit: Bring your AI chat to life with interactive Google Maps experiences
sources.snippet:
Blog: Introducing Contextual View in Google Maps AI Kit: Bring your AI chat to life with interactive Google Maps experiences –Google Maps Platform
Introducing new AI-powered products and tools from Google Maps Platform.[Learn more](https://mapsplatform.google.com/ai).
![Close](https://mapsplatform.google.com/static/images/icons/menu-close-white.svg)
New! Subscribe to save with one monthly price and access more products.[Learn more.](https://mapsplatform.google.com/pricing/)
![Close](https://mapsplatform.google.com/static/images/icons/menu-close-white.svg)
# Introducing Contextual View in Google Maps AI Kit: Bring your AI chat to life with interactive Google Maps experiences
![Twitter](https://mapsplatform.google.com/static/images/icons/social-twitter.svg)![LinkedIn](https://mapsplatform.google.com/static/images/icons/social-linkedin.svg)![Facebook](https://mapsplatform.google.com/static/images/icons/social-facebook.svg)[![Email](https://mapsplatform.google.com/static/images/icons/social-email.svg)](#)
James Harrison
Product Manager, Google Maps Platform
Ahsan Ashraf
Product Marketing Manager, Google Maps Platform
Nov 10, 2025
![Twitter](https://mapsplatform.google.com/static/images/icons/social-twitter.svg)![LinkedIn](https://mapsplatform.google.com/static/images/icons/social-linkedin.svg)![Facebook](https://mapsplatform.google.com/static/images/icons/social-facebook.svg)[![Email](https://mapsplatform.google.com/static/images/icons/social-email.svg)](#)
Today, we're announcing[Contextual View](https://mapsplatform.google.com/ai), a new low-code component of our Google Maps AI Kit, a collection of familiar and trusted UI components from Google Maps Platform designed to bring your AI responses to life. It is designed to enrich your generative AI applications with trusted, interactive Google Maps information to create more engaging, informative, and helpful experiences.
For developers building the next generation of generative AI agents, providing users with clear, useful, and engaging information is more important than ever. But text-based responses often fall short. Users experience "text wall fatigue" and lack the visual context needed to understand what places are really like or how they relate to one another.
Contextual View is a low-code, out-of-the-box solution that can be implemented with just a few lines of code. It allows the Large Language Model (LLM), e.g. Gemini via Vertex AI, to dynamically surface specific Google Maps data and render key UI elements directly within your AI chat experience, all based on user queries and context.
![Enable LLMs](https://storage.googleapis.com/gweb-mapsplatform-cdn/uploads/lq6qgs6wvqjt-1KMVgUDkUS3fcumgAnQjl7-1825d279f2154546d6da3cacd78f9f82-ai_innovation-img_2.gif) 
Enable LLMs to display interactive maps and Places data directly within AI chat using a few lines of code with Contextual View, a Google Maps AI Kit component.
### Build richer generative AI experiences
With the Contextual View component, you can ground your AI chat experiences in rich, real-world information from Google Maps. The component gives your AI agent tools to access Google’s trusted data and visualize it for your users. Based on user intent, the LLM can control and display rich Places data, interactive 2D and 3D maps with markers, and provide contextual information like photos and user-generated content (e.g. reviews).
### Transform conversations into confident decisions
You can move beyond frustrating text-walls and allow users to see what a place is really like. By providing rich, visual context and intuitive map-based interactions, you can minimize ambiguity and enable users to make faster, more confident decisions, all within the natural flow of a conversation.
For example, a travel company can use the Contextual View to allow its AI to surface relevant visuals and information about places, helping travelers with questions about an area instead of just providing a text response.
### Develop faste

# MetaMask Announces Free Solana ID Minting for Users Before Breakpoint 2025
MetaMask is offering free Solana ID minting until December 22, unlocking exclusive Solana ecosystem perks for users.
![Kelvin Munene](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/d4ea74978aea7ee299447dc24a59aa1dac1fdc2a31a46fbf19da2cdce3d322bd?s=32&r=g)By[Kelvin Munene](https://coincentral.com/author/kelvin/)December 8, 2025**4 Mins Read
![](https://coincentral.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/08/metamask-1.png "MetaMask Announces Free Solana ID Minting for Users Before Breakpoint 2025")
## TLDR
* MetaMask users can mint a Solana ID for free until December 22, 2025.
* The initiative provides users access to perks like staking boosts and rewards.
* Solana partners like Orca and Sanctum offer exclusive benefits for Solana ID holders.
* The program aims to increase wallet adoption and engagement in Solana apps.
MetaMaskhas announced a new initiative that offers users the chance to mint a free Solana ID. This special promotion is available until December 22, 2025. The move is part of a broader partnership between MetaMask and Solana Identity, ahead of Solana’s annual developer conference, Breakpoint 2025. This initiative is designed to encourage users to engage more with Solana’s ecosystem and to provide them with exclusive benefits.
**Mint a Solana ID to Unlock Exclusive Ecosystem Perks**
By minting a Solana ID using MetaMask,[users](https://x.com/MetaMask/status/1998060127950409942?s=20)can unlock various perks across the Solana blockchain ecosystem. These include rewards like staking boosts, exclusive swaps, and access to discounts. Additionally, users will be eligible to participate in raffles and earn rewards from several key partners within the Solana network. Notable Solana-based platforms involved in the program include Orca, SNS, Sanctum, Cudis, and Play Solana.
MetaMask users will not only gain access to these platform-specific rewards but will also be able to explore further opportunities within the Solana ecosystem. These perks are meant to deepen user engagement and increase wallet adoption among MetaMask users who are interacting with Solana applications.
**How the Solana ID Minting Works**
Minting a Solana ID via MetaMask is straightforward. Users simply need to connect their[MetaMask](https://coincentral.com/metamask-introduces-musd-to-simplify-crypto-payments-and-defi-access/)wallet to the Solana Identity platform and follow the steps to mint their Solana ID. The process is free, and users will retain the ID throughout their interactions with Solana-based services. Once minted, the Solana ID will be linked to the user’s MetaMask wallet, allowing them to access the various ecosystem benefits.
The initiative aims to bring together the strengths of MetaMask’s popular walle

editorial.headline: Hivemapper Pivots to Enterprise Fleets; Seidman Hints at Shopify Stablecoin Move
editorial.standfirst: With 36% of the global road network mapped, Hivemapper hits the 'crypto ceiling' and shifts strategy to commercial fleets. CEO Ariel Seidman also dropped a potential alpha leak regarding Shopify's stablecoin roadmap.
key_takeaways: Global Coverage at 36%: Network has mapped over 60M km; customers include Lyft, NBC, and HERE Technologies (BMW/Audi).
Strategic Pivot: Hivemapper is moving beyond retail crypto incentives to equip commercial fleets (trucking/delivery) for coverage in 'non-crypto' regions.
Potential Alpha: Seidman accidentally teased a Shopify stablecoin development, suggesting a possible expansion to Solana following their earlier integration with Base.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
### The Crypto Incentive Ceiling

Hivemapper has arguably achieved what few DePIN projects have: genuine product-market fit with enterprise customers like **Lyft** and **HERE Technologies** (owned by **BMW**, **Audi**, and **Daimler**). Yet, CEO Ariel Seidman was remarkably candid at Solana Breakpoint 2025: token incentives alone cannot map the world. While the network has successfully captured 36% of the global road network—primarily in tech-forward hubs—it struggles to penetrate the "long tail" of rural geographies where crypto adoption is nonexistent. "If the crypto industry was 10x bigger, maybe tokens would work everywhere," Seidman noted. "But right now, there isn't a crypto native in a small town in Brazil waiting to map a back road."

### The 'Trojan Horse' Strategy

To bridge the gap between 36% and the target of 90% coverage, Hivemapper is pivoting toward a B2B hardware model. The company plans to deploy dashcams into commercial fleets—trucking, logistics, and delivery services—by selling the hardware as a safety and monitoring tool. These fleet operators are indifferent to **HONEY** tokens; they purchase the device to monitor driver behavior and route efficiency. The mapping data becomes a passive byproduct, effectively subsidizing the network's expansion into "data deserts" without relying on speculative retail interest. This shift represents a maturation of the DePIN model: using crypto rails for settlement and trust, but abstracting them away for the end-user to achieve scale.

### Alpha: The Shopify Slip-Up

In a moment of unscripted candor, Seidman appeared to leak details regarding **Shopify’s** stablecoin strategy. While discussing the need for "real products" over "casinos," he referenced his brother, a Shopify employee, and hinted at upcoming stablecoin features before catching himself: "Hopefully that doesn't break any news... shit." Given that Shopify integrated USDC on Coinbase's **Base** network earlier this year, Seidman’s hesitation at a Solana conference suggests a potential multi-chain expansion or a deeper, native integration on Solana is imminent. For institutional investors, this signals that major fintech infrastructure is continuing to converge with high-throughput blockchains, regardless of market sentiment.
